title: Adam Steele: Loganix Unmanaged Services Discovery
meeting_title: Jason Gong and Adam Steele: Loganix Unmanaged Services Discovery

transcript.text: I'm in Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada.
What about you?
Well, we're Canadian.
I'm in Oakland, in the Bay Area, but I'm from Toronto.
Oh, wonderful.
Wonderful.
What brought you down to Oakland?
I was just tech, you know.
Yeah, fair enough.
What do they call that?
Brain?
We're basically bleeding.
Brain drain.
There it is.
Brain drain.
I think there's another term for it now.
Maybe.
Anyway, it's definitely real.
Yes, sir.
It's very real.
Cool.
It was great to meet you.
Bronco said lots of great things about you guys.
Oh, that's nice of them.
Maybe I can give a quick intro and then just tell you what we're doing.
I mean, I think I roughly understand, you know, what you guys do, but I would love to hear.
It's pretty straightforward what we do, yeah.
Yeah, and I know there's a big there's a big spectrum there.
So we'd love to learn more.
basically, GrowthX, where you can think of us, basically is an extension of a company's growth team.
Generally, companies like Webflow, AppNormal Securities, Ramp, Reddit, even, they come to us with some function of their growth they want to just hand off to us.
And our pitch is essentially, we've got the experts in most domains to build these AI plus human systems that'll deliver great outcomes.
Most of our customers do something...
a growth engine for them of some sort, an AI-powered growth engine of some sort.
Exactly.
Okay, got it.
I feel like the space now, you see a lot of products.
Usually, they're pitched like, this is magical, you use it, and your life will be better.
But the reality is, like, most of the time, you need somebody good executing, you know, to set it up and work the tools.
Especially if it's AI, you need, like, these weird unicorn people who are, like, half technical, and then half they also understand the...
So they don't shoot themselves in the foot.
Yeah, I feel that.
I find that that's something we struggle with, or we've struggled with in probably the last year or so as we've tried to adopt AI in different ways, especially as it relates to our own growth.
Yeah, for sure.
I mean, I don't know what flavor.
Nobody's an expert, right?
So we're all just sort of figuring it out.
And it's just it's the right sort of combination of you're really into AI and you're trying to push the boundaries and you understand its capabilities.
But you can then actually make it useful.
They can actually provide a business value.
And oftentimes I find team members have lost the forest for the trees in that pursuit.
They just create a really cool thing that doesn't really, maybe it saves us the money, but it doesn't make us any money.
I think with these tools, like when you don't understand the guts of it, like part of your brain just kind of turns off.
It even happens with our team.
Like we have these workflows, you know, like we generate content.
Right.
But like there's people in the middle to kind of steer it.
And then there's a final editor at the end.
But like sometimes I'm really surprised by like these amazing writers and SEO people.
They'll like run the workflow and they won't even look at it and they'll just trust that it's magical.
So yeah, but basically that's what we do for our clients.
So essentially for a lot of these kind of pretty big companies, we own their like a chunk of their SEO function.

And as a part of that, you know, a lot of them are interested in increasing their kind of domain authority and, you know, creating backlinks to certain pages to try to get tactical about, you know, hitting higher rankings for certain keywords.
And right now, yeah, I mean, we basically just have a need for it.
Like we, we thought about and tried doing it in-house.
It's similar for why our customers don't do what we do in-house.
Like I think link building is just its own little beast.
And like, I don't think it, like, I think, you know, I would much rather partner with somebody that knows how to do it.
Um, just cause at least to me, it seems so kind of like relationship driven in a lot of ways.
And also just like a different, you know, fundamental set of things to do.
It's like cold email outreach, but you have to build and maintain a relationship with each one of those people that you, that, you know, replies to any one of your campaigns.
Right.
And make sure that all their information is up to date and trust that it actually is.
And when it isn't, negotiate a solution and so on and so forth.
I feel like there's so many tactics in there.
then I feel like just over time, that network you build, like for us to just build that and get the same efficiency that you guys have.
It's just like, I don't know if it's, I don't know if that's like one of the things we want to be good at, you know, of all the things that we have to figure out to make this company work.
Well, I can speak very personally to it because it wasn't always our core competency.
In fact, for the longest time we went to brokers and resold.
And so we found folks that had those relationships directly with.
And so we had kind of a cheat code for the first five to eight years of our business.
And it's really only in the last three to four years of our business that we'd have to figure that out because the brokers were just too damn unreliable.
And it was just, you spoke to them and then they spoke to the publisher and then the person on the publisher's side.
then that person, the telephone, it was horrible.
And it just created really slow turnaround times, very poor reliability, just problems and the extra costs you're paying for the broker's fee, too.
So we had to largely reinvent ourselves and figure this all out for ourselves and getting that cost down to where your cost per, like we call it, you know, cost per publisher or cost per partner.
Down to the point where it's so low that even if you don't ever sell a link on that site, you sell enough links on enough of the sites that you onboarded as partners, it's still math.
That's the challenging bit because not every link is going to – and heck, I mean, even in-house, not every link is going to help.
You just have to be okay with that.
Only a percentage of them are actually going to help.
So it's like cost per partner that is valuable and moves the needle.
It's not a fun thing to try and get right.
It's easy, but it's tough, if that makes sense.
It's easy, but it's simple, or I can't remember what the saying is.
It's easy, but it's not simple, or some version of that.
In any event, yeah, trying to do it in-house is a pain in the .
And mind you, I guess I'm a bit biased, but yeah, it's a pain.
Yeah, and I mean, I think where we deliver value is being the part that links all this together and being their kind of strategic partner.
Our customers don't care if we do it ourselves or we go out and buy it.
They just pay us to deliver an outcome.

I feel like building links is probably not super high on that list just because folks like you exist and offer them.

You could have a partner like us for a time and then slowly build your own in-house team to do the same or supplement in some way.
That's super, super common.
Our clients like Kraken or HubSpot, I think they all started.
know HubSpot already had something of a team, but it's largely grown since.
HubSpot?

Tony Go?
Tony, yeah.

Sites that apparently they can just get a link on and never bother to ask them.
brokered with either.

Okay.

So, so, so,

This is my, one of my deliverables for the quarter or whatever it might be.
They're coming to us with this.
They have a strategy themselves or it's, I am a part of their strategy.
Generally, I am one piece of an overall strategy and they're telling us, you know, what anchors they want.
They're telling us where they want to point them or what waiting to apply.
want five links to this URL, three links to this URL, one to this URL, blah, blah, blah, And, and they say, can you do this?
And so then we go into our list and we say, and we look around and say, okay, well, um, you know, we've, we've probably got about three months worth of runway.
We do this for about three months right now.
And we'll just start doing our own, basically, basically take a pod and outreach pod and dedicate it towards building more relationships in that niche.
And just, you know, kind of build as many as fast as we can, so that when we get to the end of the runway, we've got, you know, more ready to go.
And so that's how those relationships basically exist.
It's very, very custom.
It's quite managed, meaning you're not just going into the dashboard and ordering whatever the hell you want.

So, yeah, I mean, I don't know.

I'll be

Like we kind of try to think about it like that.
I understand.

be a part of that strategy.

don't have the knowledge.

top.
the table.

then the reason we're venture-funded is that's not the point of GrowthX.

hopefully that's more interesting.

We are are are

What changes is you, we figure out how much support, which generally translates to hours you need from say an AM or.

p.m.
time on a monthly basis.

you're to get response.

Traffic, how, whatever.

I need at least this many links.

Sheet, and you're approving.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Explore potential partnership between GrowthX.ai and Loganix for link building and SEO services.](https://fathom.video/share/1Hz23ryb_rAyzmxLyeQ2-Msh-cKs_r9_?tab=summary&timestamp=1.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [GrowthX.ai seeks a reliable link building partner for their clients, ranging from Series A startups to large companies like Reddit and Webflow](https://fathom.video/share/1Hz23ryb_rAyzmxLyeQ2-Msh-cKs_r9_?tab=summary&timestamp=4.0)
  - [Loganix offers flexible link building services, from transactional to strategic partnerships, with pricing ranging from \~$200-$1000+ per link](https://fathom.video/share/1Hz23ryb_rAyzmxLyeQ2-Msh-cKs_r9_?tab=summary&timestamp=529.0)
  - [Loganix can provide additional SEO services like keyword research, local SEO, and content creation as needed](https://fathom.video/share/1Hz23ryb_rAyzmxLyeQ2-Msh-cKs_r9_?tab=summary&timestamp=509.0)
  - [Typical turnaround time for standard link building is 8-10 days, with more complex requests taking longer](https://fathom.video/share/1Hz23ryb_rAyzmxLyeQ2-Msh-cKs_r9_?tab=summary&timestamp=772.0)

## Topics

### GrowthX.ai's Business Model and Needs

  - [AI-powered growth agency serving B2B clients like Webflow, AppNormal Securities, Ramp, and Reddit](https://fathom.video/share/1Hz23ryb_rAyzmxLyeQ2-Msh-cKs_r9_?tab=summary&timestamp=62.0)
  - [Offers end-to-end SEO services, including link building, which they currently outsource](https://fathom.video/share/1Hz23ryb_rAyzmxLyeQ2-Msh-cKs_r9_?tab=summary&timestamp=62.0)
  - [Seeking a reliable partner for consistent, high-quality link building across multiple clients](https://fathom.video/share/1Hz23ryb_rAyzmxLyeQ2-Msh-cKs_r9_?tab=summary&timestamp=60.0)
  - [Flexible on engagement model, from transactional to strategic partnership](https://fathom.video/share/1Hz23ryb_rAyzmxLyeQ2-Msh-cKs_r9_?tab=summary&timestamp=638.0)

### Loganix's Service Offerings

  - [Core business (70%) is link building, with additional SEO services available](https://fathom.video/share/1Hz23ryb_rAyzmxLyeQ2-Msh-cKs_r9_?tab=summary&timestamp=509.0)
  - [Two main engagement models:](https://fathom.video/share/1Hz23ryb_rAyzmxLyeQ2-Msh-cKs_r9_?tab=summary&timestamp=1217.0)
    1.  Transactional: Fixed-price "authority links" ($400-$600 per link)
    2.  Strategic Partnership: Publisher fee plus margin, more flexible pricing ($200-$1000+ per link)
  - [Customizable workflow integration (Asana, spreadsheets, Notion, etc.)](https://fathom.video/share/1Hz23ryb_rAyzmxLyeQ2-Msh-cKs_r9_?tab=summary&timestamp=1174.0)
  - [Additional services: local SEO, keyword research, content creation](https://fathom.video/share/1Hz23ryb_rAyzmxLyeQ2-Msh-cKs_r9_?tab=summary&timestamp=1604.0)

### Link Building Process and Expectations

  - [Average turnaround time: 8-10 days for standard requests](https://fathom.video/share/1Hz23ryb_rAyzmxLyeQ2-Msh-cKs_r9_?tab=summary&timestamp=1745.0)
  - [Longer timelines for specialized niches (e.g., European markets, crypto)](https://fathom.video/share/1Hz23ryb_rAyzmxLyeQ2-Msh-cKs_r9_?tab=summary&timestamp=1817.0)
  - [Flexible approval process: client can review and approve each link or set criteria for automatic approval](https://fathom.video/share/1Hz23ryb_rAyzmxLyeQ2-Msh-cKs_r9_?tab=summary&timestamp=1167.0)
  - [Loganix can source new publishers for specific client needs](https://fathom.video/share/1Hz23ryb_rAyzmxLyeQ2-Msh-cKs_r9_?tab=summary&timestamp=1531.0)

### Pricing and Quality Considerations

  - [Link prices vary based on metrics (Domain Rating, traffic, etc.) and publisher relationships](https://fathom.video/share/1Hz23ryb_rAyzmxLyeQ2-Msh-cKs_r9_?tab=summary&timestamp=1217.0)
  - [Higher-end clients (e.g., HubSpot) may require more authoritative links (DR 70+) for competitive keywords](https://fathom.video/share/1Hz23ryb_rAyzmxLyeQ2-Msh-cKs_r9_?tab=summary&timestamp=1408.0)
  - [Loganix recommends against very low-cost links ($190) for most clients, as they may not be effective](https://fathom.video/share/1Hz23ryb_rAyzmxLyeQ2-Msh-cKs_r9_?tab=summary&timestamp=1380.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Jason to send Loganix specific link building requirements and objectives for GrowthX.ai clients](https://fathom.video/share/1Hz23ryb_rAyzmxLyeQ2-Msh-cKs_r9_?tab=summary&timestamp=1572.0)
  - [Jason to explore potential keyword research project with Loganix](https://fathom.video/share/1Hz23ryb_rAyzmxLyeQ2-Msh-cKs_r9_?tab=summary&timestamp=1624.0)
  - [Loganix to provide detailed pricing and service options based on GrowthX.ai's needs](https://fathom.video/share/1Hz23ryb_rAyzmxLyeQ2-Msh-cKs_r9_?tab=summary&timestamp=1697.0)
  - [If moving forward, expect 1-week onboarding process followed by active link building in week 2](https://fathom.video/share/1Hz23ryb_rAyzmxLyeQ2-Msh-cKs_r9_?tab=summary&timestamp=1745.0)
